source_paper_title: Congruency-Constrained TU Problems Beyond the Bimodular Case
source_paper_bibtex: @article{N_gele_2024, title={Congruency-Constrained TU Problems Beyond the Bimodular Case}, volume={49}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2023.1381}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2023.1381}, number={3}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Nägele, Martin and Santiago, Richard and Zenklusen, Rico}, year={2024}, month=aug, pages={1303–1348} }

Page 2, Section 1 (Introduction)

In particular, there is a long-standing open question on whether ILPs are efficiently solvable if their constraint matrix is \(\Delta\)-modular for constant \(\Delta\).
title: Polynomial-time solvability of integer programs with bounded subdeterminants setting
background_latex:
An integer linear program (ILP) is
\[\min\{c^\top x: Ax\le b,\ x\in\mathbb Z^n\},\]
where \(A\in\mathbb Z^{k\times n}\), \(b\in\mathbb Z^k\), and \(c\in\mathbb Z^n\). For \(\Delta\in\mathbb Z_{>0}\), a matrix \(A\in\mathbb Z^{k\times n}\) is called \(\Delta\)-modular if:
1) \(A\) has full column rank \(n\); and
2) every \(n\times n\) submatrix \(B\) of \(A\) satisfies \(|\det(B)|\le \Delta\).
Equivalently, the maximum absolute value of an \(n\times n\) subdeterminant of \(A\) is at most \(\Delta\).

A special and classical case is \(\Delta=1\), where \(A\) is totally unimodular (TU): every square submatrix has determinant in \(\{-1,0,1\}\). In that case, linear optimization over \(\{x\in\mathbb R^n: Ax\le b\}\) yields an integral optimal solution whenever \(b\) is integral.

The paper studies an important subclass of bounded-subdeterminant ILPs: congruency-constrained TU problems, i.e., TU systems plus one congruency constraint \(\gamma^\top x\equiv r\pmod m\). It proves feasibility can be decided in strongly polynomial randomized time for modulus \(m=3\), and more generally for prime \(m\) when all but at most two residues are allowed.

Despite this progress, the broad question for general \(\Delta\)-modular matrices remains unresolved.
problem_definition_latex:
Design an algorithm that, for every fixed constant \(\Delta\in\mathbb Z_{>0}\), solves in time polynomial in the encoding length of \((A,b,c)\) the ILP
\[\min\{c^\top x: Ax\le b,\ x\in\mathbb Z^n\},\]
under the promise that \(A\in\mathbb Z^{k\times n}\) is \(\Delta\)-modular (i.e., has full column rank and all \(n\times n\) subdeterminants have absolute value at most \(\Delta\)).

An integer linear program (ILP) is
\[\min\{c^\top x: Ax\le b,\ x\in\mathbb Z^n\},\]
where \(A\in\mathbb Z^{k\times n}\), \(b\in\mathbb Z^k\), and \(c\in\mathbb Z^n\). For \(\Delta\in\mathbb Z_{>0}\), a matrix \(A\in\mathbb Z^{k\times n}\) is called \(\Delta\)-modular if:
1) \(A\) has full column rank \(n\); and
2) every \(n\times n\) submatrix \(B\) of \(A\) satisfies \(|\det(B)|\le \Delta\).
Equivalently, the maximum absolute value of an \(n\times n\) subdeterminant of \(A\) is at most \(\Delta\).

A special and classical case is \(\Delta=1\), where \(A\) is totally unimodular (TU): every square submatrix has determinant in \(\{-1,0,1\}\). In that case, linear optimization over \(\{x\in\mathbb R^n: Ax\le b\}\) yields an integral optimal solution whenever \(b\) is integral.

The paper studies an important subclass of bounded-subdeterminant ILPs: congruency-constrained TU problems, i.e., TU systems plus one congruency constraint \(\gamma^\top x\equiv r\pmod m\). Design an algorithm that, for every fixed constant \(\Delta\in\mathbb Z_{>0}\), solves in time polynomial in the encoding length of \((A,b,c)\) the ILP
\[\min\{c^\top x: Ax\le b,\ x\in\mathbb Z^n\},\]
under the promise that \(A\in\mathbb Z^{k\times n}\) is \(\Delta\)-modular (i.e., has full column rank and all \(n\times n\) subdeterminants have absolute value at most \(\Delta\)).
open_problem_quote: In particular, there is a long-standing open question on whether ILPs are efficiently solvable if their constraint matrix is \(\Delta\)-modular for constant \(\Delta\).
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The statement asks for an efficient algorithm for all instances with a given bounded-subdeterminant parameter, so a single finite counterexample instance is not meaningful unless one targets a specific proposed algorithm or complexity hypothesis. Automated search could generate hard instances, but there is no crisp, efficiently checkable property whose violation would refute the existence of some (unknown) polynomial-time algorithm.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: One can encode candidate algorithms or reductions for restricted subclasses of \(\Delta\)-modular ILPs and score them by empirical success on generated instances and runtime proxies, potentially discovering useful heuristics or structural decompositions. However, converting such discoveries into a general polynomial-time algorithm (or hardness proof) requires substantial theoretical insight beyond what a computable fitness signal can reliably guide.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The open problem asks for a polynomial-time algorithm (for each fixed constant \(\Delta\)) for ILPs \(\min\{c^\top x: Ax\le b,\ x\in\mathbb Z^n\}\) under the promise that \(A\) has full column rank and all \(n\times n\) subdeterminants are bounded by \(\Delta\). Beyond the totally unimodular case \(\Delta=1\), the main algorithmic breakthrough remains the bimodular case \(\Delta=2\): Artmann--Weismantel--Zenklusen give a strongly polynomial algorithm by reducing to parity-constrained TU problems and exploiting Seymour-type decompositions, building on structural reductions for bimodular matrices due to Veselov--Chirkov. This establishes that bounded subdeterminants can yield tractability, but only at \(\Delta=2\) in full generality.

The source paper (Nägele--Santiago--Zenklusen) advances a different but closely related frontier: TU systems plus one congruency constraint \(\gamma^\top x\equiv r\pmod m\). For prime \(m\), such problems are equivalent (via standard transformations) to ILPs whose full-dimensional subdeterminants lie in \(\{0,\pm m\}\), i.e., a structured \(\Delta\)-modular setting with \(\Delta=m\). Their randomized strongly polynomial feasibility algorithm for \(m=3\) (and more generally for prime \(m\) when all but at most two residues are allowed) relies on a decomposition lemma for TU polyhedra, flatness/proximity bounds, and reductions on base blocks to congruency-constrained submodular minimization (using Nägele--Sudakov--Zenklusen) and to exact-length circulation (using Camerini--Galbiati--Maffioli). This shows that congruency constraints can be handled efficiently in nontrivial cases beyond parity, but it still falls short of solving general \(\Delta\)-modular ILPs.

On the broader \(\Delta\)-modular question, additional partial progress exists under further structure assumptions. Fiorini--Joret--Weltge--Yuditsky give a polynomial-time algorithm for totally \(\Delta\)-modular ILPs when each row has at most two nonzeros, via circulation-type methods with congruency/parity constraints. Complementary structural work (e.g., Lee--Paat--Stallknecht--Xu) develops proximity-style bounds for \(\Delta\)-modular IPs that may be useful for future augmentation or decomposition algorithms. Overall, no general polynomial-time algorithm is known for fixed \(\Delta\ge 3\) in the full \(\Delta\)-modular model, so the problem remains open; current techniques suggest promising directions via (i) reductions to TU plus a bounded number of congruency constraints, (ii) stronger proximity/flatness theorems for \(\Delta\)-modular polyhedra, and (iii) identifying additional decompositions that generalize Seymour’s framework beyond TU while preserving congruency-handling subroutines.

sources.title: A Strongly Polynomial Algorithm for Bimodular Integer Linear Programming
sources.theorem_or_result: Gives a strongly polynomial-time algorithm for ILPs whose full-rank constraint matrix has all full-dimensional subdeterminants bounded by \(\Delta=2\) (bimodular). Uses reduction to parity-constrained TU problems and Seymour decomposition.
sources.connection: This is the main positive result for the bounded-subdeterminant program when \(\Delta=2\), and the starting point for attempts to extend to fixed \(\Delta>2\). It also motivates studying TU systems plus one congruence constraint as a route to general \(\Delta\)-modular ILPs.

sources.title: Integer program with bimodular matrix
sources.theorem_or_result: Shows structural properties and polynomial-time solvability for bimodular matrices; implies reductions of bimodular ILPs to parity-constrained TU formulations used later in \(\Delta=2\) algorithms.
sources.connection: Provides the key reduction underpinning the \(\Delta=2\) case and highlights why extending beyond \(\Delta=2\) is nontrivial (the reduction does not extend to \(\Delta>2\)).

sources.title: Integer programs with bounded subdeterminants and two nonzeros per row
sources.theorem_or_result: Gives a polynomial-time algorithm for totally \(\Delta\)-modular ILPs when each row has at most two nonzeros, via reductions to circulation-type problems with parity/congruency constraints.
sources.connection: Represents progress for \(\Delta\)-bounded subdeterminants beyond \(\Delta=2\) under additional sparsity structure, supporting the plausibility of polynomial-time algorithms for fixed \(\Delta\) in broader settings.

sources.title: Polynomial upper bounds on the number of differing columns of \(\Delta\)-modular integer programs
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops structural/proximity-type bounds for \(\Delta\)-modular integer programs, bounding how many columns can differ between certain solutions; contributes to understanding geometry/structure under bounded subdeterminants.
sources.connection: While not an algorithmic solution, such proximity/structure results are typical ingredients in fixed-parameter or augmentation algorithms and may inform future polynomial-time approaches for fixed \(\Delta\).

sources.title: Submodular Minimization Under Congruency Constraints
sources.theorem_or_result: Provides algorithms for minimizing submodular functions under a congruency constraint \(|S|\equiv r\pmod m\) for constant prime power \(m\).
sources.connection: Used as a key subroutine in Nägele--Santiago--Zenklusen to handle base-block TU matrices (transpose network matrices) under congruency constraints; illustrates how congruency constraints can be handled in special TU-structured settings, relevant to extending beyond \(\Delta=2\).

sources.title: Random Pseudo-Polynomial Algorithms for Exact Matroid Problems
sources.theorem_or_result: Gives randomized pseudo-polynomial algorithms for exact matroid problems; includes an algorithmic approach to exact-length circulation (and related exact weight problems).
sources.connection: Nägele--Santiago--Zenklusen reduce congruency-constrained circulation subproblems (arising from network-matrix TU blocks) to exact-length circulation, invoking this randomized machinery; this is part of the current best progress for modulus \(m=3\) feasibility.

sources.title: A Strongly Polynomial Algorithm to Solve Combinatorial Linear Programs
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces a strongly polynomial algorithm for a broad class of linear programs with small coefficients (combinatorial LPs).
sources.connection: Serves as a foundational tool repeatedly used in bounded-subdeterminant and TU-congruency work to solve relaxations and optimize widths/projections in strongly polynomial time; underlies several structural lemmas in the source paper.
